video_title: Trinity Wall Street's Nicholas Csicsko: How Transparency Survives a 50% Drawdown
gist: Nicholas Csicsko of Trinity Wall Street's endowment explains how prioritizing manager transparency and relationship depth lets an allocator survive severe drawdowns (including a manager that fell ~50% before compounding 5x), and details his frameworks for manager selection, portfolio tilt management, exit discipline, and his skepticism about private-market marks and illiquidity in today's environment.

insights.claim: Building deep transparency and trust with managers in good times creates durable relationships that survive severe drawdowns, effectively transforming capital from fungible money into a resilient partnership.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Allocators should engineer transparency proactively so relationships hold when performance breaks down.
insights.quote: in that way, all of a sudden, capital stops being funible because it's no longer just money. It's a partnership.

insights.claim: Investors should behave in good times exactly as they would want to behave in their worst moment, using that anticipated stress as the baseline for how to communicate and treat partners.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Pre-committing to crisis-grade behavior avoids the defensive reflex that destroys relationships under stress.
insights.quote: if you imagine yourself in your worst moment, that's how you behave in the good times.

insights.claim: The instinctive defensive reaction of putting one's guard up under stress is usually the wrong move in investing; letting a partner see your struggles and decisions produces better outcomes.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Managers who open up during pain retain LPs; those who tense up lose them.
insights.quote: the normal reaction for people when they're challenged is to put their guard up, is to become defensive. And a lot of times I've found that the opposite is actually most successful

insights.claim: Being perceived as a top-quartile or top-decile LP partner earns tangible rewards over time, including greater access to the manager and unique co-investment or capacity opportunities.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Transparency is a two-way street; being an exceptional LP is itself an edge that compounds into deal access.
insights.quote: if we can be perceived as a top quartortile or potentially even top desile partner, we will reap those rewards over time

insights.claim: Extreme manager transparency can include a manager voluntarily emailing his entire portfolio in an Excel file every month, reflecting a total lack of concern about how the LP would use the holdings.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: The highest-trust managers volunteer full position-level disclosure, giving LPs real-time exposure insight.
insights.quote: he he would just he still does just sends uh his entire portfolio in an Excel file to to me monthly

insights.claim: A concentrated long-short manager Trinity backed in 2017 fell 14% in 2019 (then his worst year) and a further ~30% in 2020, approaching a ~50% peak-to-trough drawdown, before compounding roughly 5x from 2021 to 2026.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Enduring a near-50% drawdown with a trusted manager delivered a 5x recovery, quantifying the payoff of patience.
insights.quote: you have a draw down that's starting to get close to 50% at the trough... from 21 to 26, it's been a 5x.

insights.claim: Conviction to hold a manager through a deep drawdown rests on three factors: stability of the business, grit in the manager to keep going, and belief in the underlying holdings.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: These three tests give allocators a concrete checklist for distinguishing patience from denial during losses.
insights.quote: there need to be a few factors to have patience and that's stability of business, grit in the manager to keep going and a belief in what they own.

insights.claim: The decisive reason to hold the drawdown manager was not just belief in him but that his short-side skill and holdings served a distinct diversifying role no other portfolio position filled, which proved out as his short book thrived in 2022.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Portfolio role, not just manager affection, justifies fighting to retain an underperformer.
insights.quote: It was they served a distinct role in the portfolio and that was worth fighting for.

insights.claim: This manager deliberately capped his fund, closing to outside capital around $300 million because he never wanted to exceed roughly $500-600 million, and would proactively return capital when his desired holdings no longer fit his AUM.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Managers who cap AUM to protect strategy integrity signal alignment prized by long-term LPs.
insights.quote: this manager is the type of manager that closed outside capital when he was about at 300 million in assets because he didn't ever really want to be over 5600 million

insights.claim: In hindsight the allocator's error during the drawdown was under-adding, not over-holding; the team believed 2020 was the time to add but felt paralyzed, and later wished they had added more.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Even conviction-holders freeze at the trough; recognizing paralysis is itself an edge for adding into pain.
insights.quote: In hindsight, I really wish we would have added more, but um in those moments, you feel challenged and you feel paralyzed sometimes.

insights.claim: About half of the drawdown manager's capital redeemed during his worst year, yet the LP retained conviction because the AUM was challenged while the business itself was not.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Distinguishing AUM stress from business viability is key to holding through mass redemptions.
insights.quote: it was clear that even though the AUM was challenged, the business was not.

insights.claim: Trinity's portfolio skews toward fundamental, transparent, concentrated managers specifically so the office can easily explain to its many stakeholders where capital sits and in what businesses.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Stakeholder communication needs can rationally drive an allocator toward transparent, concentrated managers.
insights.quote: it pushed us towards managers that were transparent and managers that really often concentrated in specific holdings, all to be able to more easily speak to our stakeholders

insights.claim: The best investors can take a hundred data points and identify the one to three that actually matter, a signal-filtering skill Trinity actively tries to hone in its own team.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Prioritize hiring and developing the ability to isolate decisive variables over exhaustive data gathering.
insights.quote: The best investors we work with can take a 100red data points and tell you the one to three that they think matter.

insights.claim: There is diminishing value in evaluating the 30th fund doing the same strategy; allocators should recognize an indifference point and redirect time toward portfolio construction.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Exhaustive comp-set analysis has a stopping point; over-sourcing wastes scarce time.
insights.quote: I'm not sure there's a lot of value in looking at the 30th fund that does the same thing that you're looking at

insights.claim: Hours spent by an allocator with a manager correlate strongly with an eventual investment, creating an institutional-momentum bias that allocators must consciously check and be willing to walk away from.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Sunk time creates commitment inertia; disciplined allocators keep the option to turn away at any point.
insights.quote: there's this correlation of time... that's just this bias that we always have to check.

insights.claim: If an allocator lacks conviction from the very start of an investment, they should not make it, because that conviction is what enables holding through a manager's inevitable early rough patch.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Underwriting conviction upfront is the prerequisite for surviving early drawdowns without capitulating.
insights.quote: if you don't have that conviction from the start, you shouldn't make the investment.

insights.claim: A manager repeatedly deferring an eager LP's investment request in favor of deepening the personal relationship first signals the trust-building priority that ultimately matters most in long-duration partnerships.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: The most relationship-driven managers gate access on mutual knowledge, not eagerness or dollars.
insights.quote: he says, "Don't you think we should get to know each other better more?"

themes.theme: Transparency's Edge Cases and IR as a Weapon

insights.claim: A manager firing an analyst for repeatedly lying and then voluntarily disclosing the embarrassing full story to LPs demonstrated an ethics-driven compass, which the allocator ultimately read as greater transparency rather than a people-management deficiency.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Willingness to share ugly internal truths builds LP empathy and long-term support rather than eroding it.
insights.quote: he's just more transparent because we are it's framed to us that all these firms are perfect and professional... But inside of them are humans and humans are complicated

insights.claim: A manager's first rough patch is best experienced early in a fund's life (though not the first year), because the self-knowledge and team muscle built in that moment shape how the firm communicates and survives future stress.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Battle-tested early managers who kept their culture become durable holdings; smooth early runs mask untested fragility.
insights.quote: it would be for a PM to have their rough patch or one of their rough patches early in the fund's life. hopefully not in the first year

insights.claim: A manager who had a great 10-year run then faltered, tensed up, stopped sharing with LPs, and took non-LP-friendly liquidity actions became untenable to hold, because doubt among the team and investors destroys patience instantly.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Guarded, non-LP-friendly behavior under stress is a decisive exit trigger regardless of past track record.
insights.quote: at the moment that the institutional base, the team, the investor starts to doubt the relationship, the patience just disappears.

insights.claim: Treating investor relations as an ancillary rather than front-line function wastes a critical resource; firms that empower IR (e.g., a COO who began in IR) optimize their whole team and reap real returns.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Allocators can read a manager's ego and durability through how seriously the firm invests in IR.
insights.quote: when you view your IRBD as an ancillary function you're missing a really big resource

insights.claim: A cyclically-focused equity fund delivered only mid-single-digit returns for its first five years while markets compounded at high-single to low-double digits, yet outperformed its own sectors and later ran 6x from 2020 because it was battle-tested early and kept its culture.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Sector-adjusted outperformance during a muted absolute run can precede a large recovery for a resilient firm.
insights.quote: The run since 2020 has been a 6x... it's because they were battle tested early and built a business there and they haven't lost that culture.

insights.claim: A firm's willingness to let individual analysts spend unscripted time alone with investors at annual meetings signals authentic trust in their people and is a positive due-diligence tell.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Unscripted analyst access is a low-cost signal of genuine culture and internal trust.
insights.quote: there's no script, there's no prompt... They just show that they trust their people.

insights.claim: Transparency need not mean disclosing every short; effective managers communicate a short book at the level of themes or sectors so LPs can understand and support it without exposing individual positions.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Thematic-level disclosure lets LPs back a short book without compromising the manager's edge.
insights.quote: it's making sure you communicate that at a level like uh say themes or sectors or anything so that someone can understand and support it

insights.claim: Trinity actively guards against a bias toward smaller managers by deliberately diversifying across firm age and size, having built a strong high-transparency relationship with a well-known spin-out that now manages over $30 billion.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Large funds can sustain transparency through group dinners and robust IR teams, so allocators shouldn't reflexively avoid scale.
insights.quote: they sit at over 30 billion in assets today... What the firm's done as it's grown is figure out a way to keep that kind of connectivity and transparency.

insights.claim: More than half of an institutional allocator's job is being a portfolio manager, working to make the money-weighted return exceed the time-weighted return by adding to what's not working and trimming what is.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Edge comes as much from position sizing and rebalancing discipline as from picking the best managers.
insights.quote: working every day to make sure your money weighted return is better than your time weighted return.

insights.claim: Managers supply unique betas but have no idea what the aggregate portfolio looks like, so managing the top-down tilts those betas create is the allocator's responsibility, not the managers'.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Bottom-up manager selection must be reconciled into deliberately managed aggregate tilts.
insights.quote: how they work together is your responsibility not theirs.

insights.claim: Allocators should be wary of making too many top-down macro calls because they lack the tools and expertise, so tilt management should be marginal adjustments referenced against a benchmark rather than active macro bets.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Discipline against over-active top-down calls protects allocators from playing a game they can't win.
insights.quote: the thing you definitely want to check is people in our seat making too many top- down calls because we do not have the tools to do that.

insights.claim: High manager transparency gives Trinity near-daily P&L and mid-month exposure visibility, which is especially valuable in volatile months to know real-time positioning rather than stale month-end data, even though it shouldn't change long-term actions.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Position-level transparency yields real-time aggregate risk visibility that improves tilt management timing.
insights.quote: it's very nice to know where you are in real time, not where you were at last month's end.

insights.claim: Benchmark-relative investing has been especially hard recently because of index concentration, forcing allocators to actively decide how to address underweights or overweights to the benchmark's top holdings.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Index concentration makes benchmark-aware public books structurally harder to outperform.
insights.quote: benchmarks have been especially especially hard in in in recent years because of the concentration.

insights.claim: A 5% position and a 5-basis-point position often get the same airtime at investment committee, so Trinity deliberately reframes decisions in basis points rather than raw dollars to allocate attention proportionally.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Framing sizing in percent terms disciplines committee attention against dollar-anchoring bias.
insights.quote: a 5% position and a five basis point position can often get the same amount of airtime at an internal investment committee meeting. And and that's not necessarily the way it should be.

insights.claim: Allocators exit too late in most cases; when something turns and fails to improve, hindsight shows they should have left earlier, and exit decisions should therefore happen faster than add decisions.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Structural bias toward holding means allocators should bias exits earlier and faster.
insights.quote: In hindsight, we exit too late in most cases

insights.claim: Getting only six to eight of ten manager-stress decisions right can still be highly rewarding, because the winners are typically caught near the drawdown trough and deliver far larger upside than the losers cost.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: The asymmetric payoff of trough-timed holds means a sub-perfect hit rate still compounds favorably.
insights.quote: if we can get six to seven to eight of those calls right, I think we end up being rewarded significantly

insights.claim: Consistency between mean-reversion and momentum orientation is critical, because oscillating between the two philosophies sets an allocator up to always miss the turning point.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Pick a mean-reversion or momentum discipline and hold it; style-switching guarantees mistimed exits.
insights.quote: when you start to go between the two, you really set yourself up to always miss the bus stop

insights.claim: Exit red flags beyond performance include increasing portfolio turnover, style drift, erratic behavior, distance to high watermark, direction of AUM, and any improper or illegal conduct which is an immediate termination.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: A concrete red-flag checklist distinguishes recoverable stress from a manager doubting himself into a spiral.
insights.quote: watching for style drift, watching for erratic behavior. All kinds of things that are telltel signs of of someone truly doubting themselves.

insights.claim: At Trinity the board delegated exit and trim decisions to not require approval before add decisions, on the logic that if you lose faith you should be out and such decisions must happen fast.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Governance should grant staff faster discretion on exits than on additions given the speed exits demand.
insights.quote: The first thing that was passed on to not need approval was exit and trim decisions.

insights.claim: When a manager is struggling Trinity conducts a formal written review, pulled forward from its periodic schedule, forcing a documented sell/hold/buy decision and testing whether holding is justified by asking why they aren't adding.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: A forced written sell/hold/buy review counters inertia and aligns the team during moments of pain.
insights.quote: if we're not adding here, why are we holding this?

themes.theme: Private Markets, Liquidity and Marks Skepticism

insights.claim: Private funds are far less reversible than public investments yet ironically offer LPs less access, often showing up saying they close in a month for a 10-to-15-year lockup, so the strategy is to spend time with them when they are not raising.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Build private relationships during non-fundraising windows since the commitment is long and irreversible.
insights.quote: private is a lot less reversible... they generally show up and say, "Hey, we're closing in a month."

insights.claim: Private markets have gotten a pass that public markets never receive, and with public markets vastly outperforming the broad private market on a Cambridge index basis, allocators are having difficult conversations about the future path of private allocations.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: The performance and scrutiny gap is prompting genuine reconsideration of private-market allocation sizing.
insights.quote: private markets have, and this is dangerous to generalize, but gotten a pass in a way that the public markets don't.

insights.claim: Private allocations are not essential and should only be held if their expected return exceeds public markets or if they provide a real diversifier, not one manufactured by smoothed quarterly marks or 'laundering of capital.'
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Privates must clear an explicit excess-return or genuine-diversification hurdle rather than earning inclusion by default.
insights.quote: It can be a diversifier as well, but it needs to be a real diversifier and not just because of uh quarterly marks or some other kind of laundering of capital.

insights.claim: With private markets unable to produce liquidity while public markets sit near all-time highs, the inability to sell now raises real questions about the validity of broad private-market marks.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: The liquidity freeze at market highs implies carried marks may be overstated across the private universe.
insights.quote: if you can't sell now, what does that say about marks on a broad basis?

insights.claim: The euphoric 2020-2021 private vintages, when funds returned to market at 12 months instead of the usual third year, may have damaged future returns, while the coming wave of record-size IPOs at roughly 2x historical volume will be a critical dynamic to watch.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Over-fast 2020-21 fundraising and an unprecedented IPO wave frame the near-term risk and opportunity for privates.
insights.quote: how the public market digests IPO volume 2x uh anything we've seen in history.

insights.claim: The contrarian view is that today's private-market stress will eventually create amazing vintages and a great opportunity to partner with groups facing less demand than they are used to and with new funds doing it the right way.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Reduced-demand and new managers in a stressed private market may offer the best future entry points.
insights.quote: at some point there'll be a great opportunity to partner with groups that maybe have a little less demand than they're used to

insights.claim: Trinity is relatively well-positioned on liquidity because it began building its private portfolio in 2016, giving it a meaningful but not overly illiquid private allocation as industry-wide liquidity has turned rough.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: A younger, more measured private program cushions an endowment against the current liquidity squeeze.
insights.quote: We're in an okay position because we started building our private portfolio in 2016.

themes.theme: Trinity's Origins and the Allocator's Mindset

insights.claim: Trinity Wall Street's endowment is a relatively new investment office built inside a 320-year-old institution, launched in 2016 by Meredith Jenkins with a fully-invested inherited portfolio to rotate.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Context: a young endowment office had to build culture and rotate an inherited book from scratch around transparency principles.
insights.quote: Trinity was this new endowment inside of a 320-year-old institution... we started really building Trinity out in 2016.

insights.claim: The best investors know themselves and spend their careers trying to stay authentic amid constant positive and negative feedback and noise, mirroring how audiences sense authenticity in music.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Self-knowledge and authenticity are underrated durable edges in an investing career full of noise.
insights.quote: the best investors I meet know themselves and they spend their whole life trying to be that authentic version.

insights.claim: The most important advice for a young allocator is to become comfortable with not knowing, because the belief that a spreadsheet or AI co-pilot holds the answer is not real.
insights.speaker: Nicholas Csicsko
insights.so_what: Embracing irreducible uncertainty, rather than seeking false precision or AI answers, makes a better investor.
insights.quote: to think that there's a spreadsheet or a co-pilot answer is just not a thing.
